opening_note: Tomatoes deserve the center of the plate. Cucumbers make everything cooler. Peaches need almost nothing. Your usual meals can stay simple. Now they taste like July.
thesis: Keep the meal. Change the season.

basket_cost.note: Expect around $30–45 for the seasonal basket, often less when produce is on sale. Proteins and pantry staples are separate; prices vary by store and region.

assumptions: One batch of rice cooked ahead
One or two proteins prepped at the start of the week
confidence.editorial_note: This score is editorial guidance, not a calculated metric. It reflects how confidently this basket supports a realistic week when combined with your usual staples.

bring_home.heading: This is what I'd bring home.
bring_home.cost_note: Around $30–45 for the seasonal basket. Proteins and pantry basics are separate.

items.name: Sweet corn
items.note: Or one bag frozen if needed.

field_notes.name: Sherry vinegar
field_notes.body: Tomatoes are already acidic. That's why sharp vinegar fights them — you're adding the same note twice. Sherry vinegar is rounder, with a quiet sweetness of its own. It cooperates. Try it on sliced tomatoes instead of lemon. A splash in the pan after chicken. A few drops in cucumber water. A mild red wine vinegar can work in its place, though the result will be a little sharper.

field_notes.name: Flaky salt goes on last
field_notes.body: Table salt dissolves into food. Flaky salt stays on the surface, so you taste it in distinct moments rather than uniformly throughout. On a ripe tomato or a peach, that difference is the whole experience. Keep a small dish of it near where you plate. It only goes on last — never while cooking. Regular salt still seasons the meal — the difference is in how you experience it on the surface.

field_notes.name: Add herbs after the heat
field_notes.body: Basil and mint lose what makes them interesting the moment heat reaches them. The oils that carry the fragrance are gone within seconds of contact with a hot pan. Add them after the heat is off, or raw, right before the plate reaches the table. The smell alone will tell you something has changed.

meal_transformations.result: It tastes like you planned it.

meal_transformations.change: Tomatoes, basil, olive oil. No jar.
Corn, tomato, cucumber instead of salsa.

weekday_dinners.name: Tomato-basil pasta
weekday_dinners.note: olive oil, garlic, no jar needed

weekend_meal.name: Tomato, burrata, basil, and sourdough.
weekend_meal.intro: One meal should feel like the reason you went shopping. This is July’s.
weekend_meal.ingredient_note: Burrata is richer and more indulgent. Fresh mozzarella is a reliable everyday alternative at about half the price — Trader Joe’s and Whole Foods both carry it.
weekend_meal.method: Slice the tomatoes thickly. Tear open the cheese. Add basil, olive oil, salt, and pepper. Use warm sourdough to drag through everything left on the plate.

drink.name: Hillcrest Spritz
drink.intro: One drink. Make it on repeat.
drink.method: Fill a rocks glass with ice. Add cherry juice and lime. Slap the mint once between your palms, drop it in, then top with sparkling water. Add more sparkling water to taste.

try_another_way.context: If basil is already out for dinner.
If there's half a cucumber left.

try_another_way.change: Muddle a ripe peach slice with the lime
try_another_way.context: If the peaches are especially ripe.

local_ritual.label: San Diego ritual
local_ritual.name: Take dinner outside.
local_ritual.description: Pack tomatoes, bread, fruit, and something cold to drink. Eat near the water while the light is still hanging around.

one_thing_to_notice.headline: The smell of a tomato vine.
one_thing_to_notice.body: Smell near the stem before you cut it. That green scent is easy to miss, and it's one of the best parts of a July tomato.

market_question: What looked best when you were shopping?

guides.name: Rosa
guides.philosophy: The best tomatoes need almost nothing.
guides.voice: Unhurried, opinionated about ripeness, quietly proud of simplicity.
guides.notices: Fragrance before color. The give of a ripe fruit under a thumb. The smell at the stem.

quotes.heirloom-tomatoes: When a tomato is right, the knife almost does it on its own.
quotes.edition: July is not the time to improve on the tomato. It is the time to get out of its way.

guides.name: Jerome
guides.voice: Direct, welcoming, practical. Has a strong opinion about which lap to buy on.
guides.notices: What is at its peak this week versus what is being pushed. What the farmers themselves are eating.

quotes.persian-cucumbers: Walk the whole market once before you reach for your wallet.
quotes.sweet-corn: The stall with the longest line is usually right. The one with the shortest is sometimes better.
quotes.edition: The best thing at the market this week is not always the most obvious thing.

guides.name: Adela
guides.philosophy: If it doesn't smell like anything, put it back.
guides.voice: Warm, precise, and slightly impatient with under-ripe fruit.
guides.notices: Fragrance first. Color second. The shoulder near the stem of a peach.

quotes.peaches: If it doesn't smell like anything, put it back.
quotes.cherries: Dark and firm. That is all you need to know.
quotes.edition: Stone fruit does not improve once it leaves the tree. Buy it ripe and eat it fast.

guides.name: Diane
guides.philosophy: One basket should carry the week.
guides.voice: Efficient, unsentimental about cooking effort, genuinely pleased by good produce doing most of the work.
guides.notices: What stretches. What combines without a recipe. What makes a Tuesday feel like something.

quotes.basil: Tear it. Don't chop it unless you have to.
quotes.mint: A few leaves change the whole bowl.
quotes.blackberries: Put them on yogurt in the morning and feel like you planned something.
quotes.edition: The week only works if you cook a little on Sunday. Everything else follows.

heirloom-tomatoes.name: Heirloom tomatoes
heirloom-tomatoes.why_now: July is peak tomato season in Southern California. Any ripe tomato — heirloom or not — is at its best right now. The heat concentrates sugars across every variety. Heirlooms are worth it if you see good ones, but a ripe vine tomato from Trader Joe's or Whole Foods will do the same work.
how_to_choose: Irregular shape is a good sign, not a flaw
Avoid anything refrigerated at the store
heirloom-tomatoes.buy_this_much: 4–6 medium tomatoes for one person for the week
heirloom-tomatoes.price_note: Vine and roma tomatoes are among the most affordable options year-round — and July is when they're actually worth buying. Heirlooms cost more but aren't required.

flavor_paths.name: Italian Summer
flavor_paths.description: Olive oil, basil, garlic, black pepper, flaky salt.

flavor_paths.name: Bright Taco
flavor_paths.description: Lime, cilantro, corn, cucumber, a little heat.

flavor_paths.name: Simple Toast
flavor_paths.description: Sourdough, olive oil, salt. The tomato does the rest.

weekday_uses: Rice bowl topping with basil
Chicken thigh salad with cucumber
Pasta sauce with garlic and olive oil
Toast with salt and olive oil
heirloom-tomatoes.weekend_use: Thick slices with burrata or fresh mozzarella, basil, olive oil, and sourdough for dragging. Burrata is richer; fresh mozzarella is half the price and nearly as good.
heirloom-tomatoes.storage: Counter only. Never refrigerate. Use within four or five days.
heirloom-tomatoes.one_thing_to_learn: Smell the stem end before you buy. A tomato that smells like a tomato at the store will taste like one at home.
heirloom-tomatoes.market_question: Which variety is ripest right now?

persian-cucumbers.name: Persian cucumbers
persian-cucumbers.why_now: Small, seedless, and thin-skinned, Persian cucumbers are at their crispest in summer. They need no peeling and almost no preparation.
persian-cucumbers.buy_this_much: 4–6 cucumbers for one person for the week
persian-cucumbers.price_note: Often one of the least expensive things in the basket. Sold in bags at most grocery stores — good value relative to how far they stretch across the week.

flavor_paths.name: Lime + mint
flavor_paths.description: Lime juice, flaky salt, fresh mint. Cool and bright.

flavor_paths.name: Yogurt + garlic
flavor_paths.description: Greek yogurt, garlic, a little olive oil. Works as a side or a sauce.

flavor_paths.name: Chili crisp + sesame
flavor_paths.description: Soy, sesame, chili crisp. Good on rice bowls and noodles.

weekday_uses: Rice bowl topping, sliced thin
Quick salad with tomato and basil
Side with salmon and mint
Wrap filling with chicken and yogurt
persian-cucumbers.weekend_use: Smashed cucumber salad with sesame, chili crisp, and scallion.
persian-cucumbers.storage: Refrigerate in a loose bag. Use within one week. Best used early in the week.
persian-cucumbers.one_thing_to_learn: Salt briefly before dressing and let the water draw out for a few minutes. The cucumber becomes more concentrated and holds its crunch longer.

peaches.name: Peaches
peaches.why_now: Southern California stone fruit peaks through July. The combination of warm days and cool nights produces fruit with real sugar and real fragrance. This is the short window.
how_to_choose: Fragrant at the stem end — this is the most reliable test
Gives slightly near the top when gently pressed
peaches.buy_this_much: 4–5 peaches for one person for the week. They do not all need to be eaten at once.
peaches.price_note: Stone fruit is most abundant and affordable in July. Peak season tends to bring prices down across most grocery stores, and peaches are often featured on sale.

flavor_paths.name: Nearly nothing
flavor_paths.description: A ripe peach over the sink. Salt if you want it.

flavor_paths.name: Mint + honey
flavor_paths.description: Sliced with fresh mint and a little honey. Breakfast or dessert.

flavor_paths.name: Savory
flavor_paths.description: Grilled or sliced alongside chicken. A little lime, a little heat.

weekday_uses: Sliced on Greek yogurt in the morning
Added to a salad with chicken or salmon
Eaten as a snack, ripe
peaches.weekend_use: Grilled peaches alongside chicken thighs with corn salad.
peaches.storage: Counter until ripe. Eat within a day or two of peak ripeness. Do not refrigerate until fully ripe, and use quickly after.
peaches.one_thing_to_learn: A peach ripens from the inside out. A soft shoulder near the stem means it is ready. A hard peach left on the counter will soften but will not improve the way tree-ripened fruit does.

sweet-corn.name: Sweet corn
sweet-corn.why_now: Corn sugars convert to starch quickly after picking. July corn eaten within a day or two of harvest is a different ingredient from corn that has traveled for a week. Grocery stores turn over fast in summer — the corn on the shelf right now is likely good.
how_to_choose: Husk tightly wrapped and bright green
Silk slightly dry at the top, not slimy
Plump kernels felt through the husk
Avoid any ears with dried-out or blackened husks
sweet-corn.price_note: One of the least expensive vegetables of summer. Widely available at any grocery store in July and often on sale.
sweet-corn.buy_this_much: 2–3 ears for one person mid-week, another 1–2 for the weekend

flavor_paths.name: Bright Taco
flavor_paths.description: Lime, taco seasoning, tomato, beans, cilantro.

flavor_paths.name: Italian Summer
flavor_paths.description: Basil, olive oil, garlic. Good off the cob into a bowl.

flavor_paths.name: Chili-crisp bowl
flavor_paths.description: Sesame, chili crisp, and a little soy over rice.

weekday_uses: Cut off the cob into a rice bowl
Added to taco bowls with beans and tomato
Quick corn and tomato salad with basil
sweet-corn.weekend_use: Corn salad alongside grilled chicken thighs and peaches.
sweet-corn.storage: Keep husked corn refrigerated and use within two days. Husked is fine for a day, unhusked is better if waiting.
sweet-corn.one_thing_to_learn: Fresh corn is good raw. Shaved directly off the cob into a salad, it has a sweetness that disappears quickly with heat.

basil.name: Basil
basil.why_now: Basil is at its most fragrant in warm weather. July heat brings out the volatile oils. The best basil right now smells like summer itself.
how_to_choose: Bright, even green leaves — no dark spots
basil.buy_this_much: One or two good bunches. It moves through a week faster than you expect.
basil.buying_note: Using basil all month? A small potted plant may last longer than a cut bunch and lets you take only what you need. A cut bunch works well when you only need it once or twice.
basil.price_note: Usually inexpensive — one of the least costly things in the basket.

flavor_paths.name: Italian Summer
flavor_paths.description: Tomato, garlic, olive oil. Torn over anything warm.

flavor_paths.name: Fresh bowl
flavor_paths.description: Rice, protein, cucumber, tomato. Basil finishes it.

flavor_paths.name: With stone fruit
flavor_paths.description: A few leaves with peach and a little salt. Unexpected and very good.

weekday_uses: Torn over rice bowls
Added to tomato and cucumber salads
Stirred into pasta with olive oil and garlic
Layered on a tomato toast
basil.weekend_use: Torn generously over tomatoes with good olive oil. This is where burrata or fresh mozzarella belongs — burrata if you want something richer, fresh mozzarella if you want something simpler.
basil.storage: Keep at room temperature with stems in water, like flowers. Do not refrigerate. Use within three or four days.
basil.one_thing_to_learn: Tear basil instead of chopping when it will be served fresh. The knife bruises it and darkens it quickly. Torn leaves hold their color and fragrance longer.

mint.name: Mint
mint.why_now: Mint is cooling by nature and perfect in summer heat. A few leaves change the temperature of a dish or a drink entirely.
how_to_choose: Strongly fragrant when a leaf is pressed
Spearmint or peppermint both work for this edition
mint.buy_this_much: One bunch. It goes further than you think.
mint.price_note: One of the least expensive things in this basket — usually $1–2 a bunch at any grocery store.

flavor_paths.name: Cucumber-mint
flavor_paths.description: Lime, salt, mint. The simplest summer side.

flavor_paths.name: Fruit and mint
flavor_paths.description: Peaches, cherries, or blackberries with a few leaves and honey.

flavor_paths.name: Spritz
flavor_paths.description: Slapped and dropped into the Hillcrest Spritz.

weekday_uses: Cucumber and mint side salad
Torn over yogurt with fruit
Added to a salmon rice bowl
Stirred into sparkling water with lime
mint.weekend_use: In the Hillcrest Spritz. Or over a peach and blackberry plate with honey.
mint.storage: Keep in water at room temperature or wrapped loosely in a damp paper towel in the refrigerator. Use within five days.
mint.one_thing_to_learn: Slapping a mint leaf between your palms before using it releases the oils without bruising the leaf. It is worth doing.

cherries.name: Cherries
cherries.why_now: California cherry season runs through July. The window is short. Buy them when they are dark and firm and eat them without much ceremony.
how_to_choose: Dark, deep color — mahogany to near-black for sweet varieties
cherries.buy_this_much: A generous pound. They disappear faster than expected.
cherries.price_note: Still a splurge compared to most things in the basket, but July is when the price drops from its winter peak. The season is short — worth it.

flavor_paths.name: As they are
flavor_paths.description: A bowl of cherries, cold, after dinner.

flavor_paths.name: With yogurt
flavor_paths.description: Halved over Greek yogurt in the morning. That is the whole thing.

flavor_paths.name: In the drink
flavor_paths.description: Tart cherry juice becomes the Hillcrest Spritz.

weekday_uses: Eaten as a snack
Halved over Greek yogurt
As the base for cherry juice in the Hillcrest Spritz
cherries.weekend_use: A plate of cherries alongside the weekend meal. No preparation needed.
cherries.storage: Refrigerate and eat within three to four days. Leave stems on until eating.
cherries.one_thing_to_learn: Tart cherry juice is a useful pantry item through July — it keeps longer than fresh cherries and makes the Hillcrest Spritz more reliable on short notice.
cherries.market_question: Which variety is ripest right now?

blackberries.name: Blackberries
blackberries.why_now: Wild and cultivated blackberries both peak in July in Southern California. They are deeply flavored, slightly tart, and good with almost nothing added.
how_to_choose: Deep, uniform black with no red druplets remaining
Check the bottom of the container for mold
A little give without being mushy
blackberries.buy_this_much: One pint. Use within two or three days.
blackberries.price_note: Peak season often brings the price down noticeably compared to off-season. Worth picking up when they look good — they're typically among the more affordable berry options in July.

flavor_paths.name: Breakfast
flavor_paths.description: Over Greek yogurt with mint and honey.

flavor_paths.name: Dessert
flavor_paths.description: With a little cream or over good vanilla ice cream.

flavor_paths.name: Salad
flavor_paths.description: In a green salad with peaches and a light vinaigrette.

weekday_uses: Greek yogurt topping in the morning
Eaten as a snack
Added to a fruit plate with peaches and mint
blackberries.weekend_use: Fruit plate with peaches, mint, and honey as a light dessert.
blackberries.storage: Refrigerate unwashed and use within two to three days. Wash only just before eating.
blackberries.one_thing_to_learn: Blackberries are fragile. They do not improve with time. Buy them close to when you will eat them and treat the container gently.
blackberries.market_question: How ripe are these right now?
